1:
"And Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men. And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids." The witnesses note Jacob seeing Esau approach with his men, and arranging his family. Gill notes Jacob, after passing over the brook and coming to his wives and children, lifted up his eyes to see if he could espy his brother coming. The witnesses note Jacob looking up and seeing Esau coming with four hundred men, and dividing the children among Leah, Rachel, and the two handmaids; the dreaded moment arrived, Jacob ordering his household for the meeting — that the long-feared encounter is at hand: "Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men. And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids"; the witnesses note the moment of truth — Jacob, having wrestled and prevailed in the night, now lifts his eyes and sees the very sight he had dreaded, "Esau... and with him four hundred men," the armed band advancing; the witnesses note Jacob's composure and care even now — he does not flee, but "divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids," arranging his household in order for the meeting; the witnesses note that this dividing was by their mothers, setting each group under its own mother for the careful and orderly approach; so that Jacob, seeing Esau come, marshals his family to meet him; he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids. The witnesses note behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men. The witnesses note And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids.

The witnesses note the dreaded moment arrived — Jacob seeing Esau advance with four hundred men, yet not fleeing but ordering his household by their mothers for the meeting; composure after the night of prevailing.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob ordering his family as Esau approaches. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, seeing the dreaded Esau come with his band, kept his composure and arranged his household for the meeting. The witnesses note the lifted up his eyes: "Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked" — Gill notes to espy his brother; the lifted up his eyes; the to espy; the looked; the his brother; the watched. The expositors observe the Esau came: "behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men" — the dreaded sight; the Esau came; the four hundred men; the the dreaded sight; the the band; the advancing. The witnesses press the not flee: Jacob does not flee; the not flee; the composure; the stands; the after prevailing; the calm. The expositors draw the lesson on the divided the children: "he divided the children" — arranged his household; the divided the children; the arranged; the his household; the in order; the by mothers. The witnesses observe the by their mothers: each group under its own mother; the by their mothers; the each group; the its mother; the orderly; the careful. The expositors note the for the meeting: marshalled to meet Esau; the for the meeting; the to meet; the Esau; the the approach; the ordered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the behold Esau came and with him four hundred men, the And he divided the children unto Leah and unto Rachel and unto the two handmaids, and the ordering of his family. The verse thus records Jacob ordering his family as Esau approaches — teaching that Jacob kept his composure and arranged his household for the meeting.
2:
"And he put the handmaids and their children foremost, and Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph hindmost." The witnesses note the order in which Jacob placed his family, by degrees of affection. Keil and Delitzsch note Jacob put the handmaids and their children foremost, Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph last. Gill notes the handmaids and their children were placed first, as being less honourable and less beloved by him. The witnesses note Jacob placing the handmaids and their children first, Leah and hers next, and Rachel and Joseph last; the careful arrangement that put the most beloved in the safest place — that Jacob ranges his family in an order that reveals his heart: "the handmaids and their children foremost, and Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph hindmost"; the witnesses note the principle of the arrangement — Jacob set them in rising order of his love and esteem, the handmaids (Bilhah and Zilpah) and their children "foremost" (first to meet Esau, and so most exposed if there were danger), then Leah and hers, and "Rachel and Joseph hindmost" (last, and so safest, the most beloved wife and son set farthest from peril); the witnesses note the tenderness and the prudence in this, that Jacob, while honouring all, placed those dearest to him where they would be least in danger should Esau prove hostile; the witnesses note that this ordering, though it shows his deeper love for Rachel and Joseph, was a father's care to shield the most beloved; so that Jacob arranges his family by degrees of affection, the dearest hindmost and safest; Rachel and Joseph hindmost. The witnesses note he put the handmaids and their children foremost. The witnesses note and Rachel and Joseph hindmost.

The witnesses note Jacob ranging his family in rising order of love — the handmaids first and most exposed, Rachel and Joseph last and safest; a father's care to shield the most beloved from peril.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrangement by degrees of affection. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob placed his family in rising order of his love, the dearest set hindmost and safest from any danger. The witnesses note the handmaids foremost: "the handmaids and their children foremost" — Gill notes as less beloved; the handmaids foremost; the less beloved; the first; the most exposed; the the handmaids. The expositors observe the Leah after: "Leah and her children after" — next in order; the Leah after; the next; the her children; the the middle; the after. The witnesses press the Rachel hindmost: "Rachel and Joseph hindmost" — last and safest; the Rachel hindmost; the last; the safest; the most beloved; the farthest. The expositors draw the lesson on the rising order: rising order of his love and esteem; the rising order; the his love; the esteem; the by degrees; the affection. The witnesses observe the shield the beloved: a father's care to shield the most beloved; the shield the beloved; the the care; the shield; the from peril; the safest. The expositors note the tenderness and prudence: tenderness joined to prudence; the tenderness and prudence; the tenderness; the prudence; the his heart; the care. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he put the handmaids and their children foremost, the and Rachel and Joseph hindmost, and the arrangement by affection. The verse thus records the arrangement by degrees of affection — teaching that Jacob placed his family in rising order of his love, the dearest hindmost and safest.
3:
"And he passed over before them, and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother." The witnesses note Jacob going before his family and bowing seven times to Esau. Gill notes Jacob passed over at the head of them, as master of the family, exposing himself to the greatest danger, to protect them and to soften his brother's mind. The witnesses note Jacob passing over before his family and bowing himself seven times to the ground until he came near Esau; the brave and humble approach, Jacob placing himself foremost and doing deep homage to his brother — that Jacob does not hide behind his household but goes first to meet the danger: "he passed over before them, and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother"; the witnesses note two graces in this act — first, his courage and self-sacrifice, that Jacob "passed over before them," putting himself at the head, foremost and most exposed, to shield his family and to be the first to meet Esau; and second, his deep humility, that he "bowed himself to the ground seven times" as he drew near, the lowliest oriental homage, a sevenfold reverence by which he abased himself before his brother to disarm his anger and win his favour; the witnesses note that Jacob, the prince who had power with God, now humbles himself to the very ground before Esau, mingling courage with the deepest submission; so that Jacob goes foremost and bows seven times before his brother; he bowed himself to the ground seven times. The witnesses note he passed over before them. The witnesses note and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.

The witnesses note Jacob going foremost to meet the danger and bowing seven times to the ground — courage and self-sacrifice joined to the deepest humility, abasing himself to disarm his brother's anger.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's brave and humble approach to Esau. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob placed himself foremost to shield his family and bowed seven times in deep homage to win his brother. The witnesses note the passed over before: "he passed over before them" — Gill notes at the head, most exposed; the passed over before; the at the head; the most exposed; the foremost; the to shield. The expositors observe the self-sacrifice: courage and self-sacrifice; the self-sacrifice; the courage; the foremost; the the danger; the shield. The witnesses press the bowed seven times: "bowed himself to the ground seven times" — the deepest homage; the bowed seven times; the deepest homage; the reverence; the sevenfold; the abased. The expositors draw the lesson on the deep humility: his deep humility before Esau; the deep humility; the humility; the abases; the to the ground; the lowly. The witnesses observe the disarm anger: to disarm Esau's anger and win favour; the disarm anger; the disarm; the win favour; the soften; the the mind. The expositors note the prince humbles: the prince who had power with God humbles himself; the prince humbles; the the prince; the power with God; the humbles; the submission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he passed over before them, the and bowed himself to the ground seven times until he came near to his brother, and the humble approach. The verse thus records Jacob's brave and humble approach — teaching that Jacob placed himself foremost and bowed seven times to win his brother.
4:
"And Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him: and they wept." The witnesses note Esau's tender and affectionate reception of Jacob. Gill notes Esau, if he rode on any creature, alighted on sight of Jacob, and to express his joy and affection made all the haste he could to meet him, as did the father of the prodigal. The witnesses note Esau running to meet Jacob, embracing him, falling on his neck, kissing him, and both weeping; the overwhelming reconciliation, Esau's heart turned to love — God's answer to Jacob's prayer — that the dreaded meeting dissolves in tears of joy: "Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him: and they wept"; the witnesses note the wonder of the change — the brother who came with four hundred men, whom Jacob feared would smite him, now "ran to meet him" in eager affection, "embraced him," "fell on his neck," "kissed him," and the two brothers "wept" together; the witnesses note that this is God's own answer to Jacob's prayer of the night, the LORD having turned Esau's heart from vengeance to love, melting his anger into tears; the witnesses note (as Gill observes) the likeness to the father of the prodigal, running, falling on the neck, and kissing; the witnesses note that, though some have suspected Esau's kiss, the commentators here take it as a genuine and God-wrought reconciliation; so that the long enmity ends in a brother's embrace and tears; Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him: and they wept. The witnesses note Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. The witnesses note and they wept.

The witnesses note Esau's tender reception — running, embracing, kissing, both weeping — God's own answer to Jacob's prayer, the LORD having turned the brother's heart from vengeance to love.

So the witnesses make the verse the overwhelming reconciliation of the brothers. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau, his heart turned by God, ran to embrace Jacob in love and tears, answering Jacob's prayer of the night. The witnesses note the ran to meet: "Esau ran to meet him" — Gill notes in eager affection; the ran to meet; the eager; the affection; the haste; the joy. The expositors observe the embraced and kissed: "embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him" — tender reception; the embraced and kissed; the tender; the the neck; the the kiss; the affection. The witnesses press the they wept: "and they wept" — both brothers wept; the they wept; the wept; the both; the tears of joy; the melted. The expositors draw the lesson on the God's answer: God's answer to Jacob's prayer; the God's answer; the the prayer; the the LORD; the turned the heart; the from vengeance. The witnesses observe the like the prodigal: like the father of the prodigal; the like the prodigal; the the father; the running; the the neck; the the kiss. The expositors note the genuine reconciliation: a genuine, God-wrought reconciliation; the genuine reconciliation; the genuine; the God-wrought; the the enmity; the ends. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Esau ran to meet him and embraced him and fell on his neck and kissed him, the and they wept, and the reconciliation. The verse thus records the overwhelming reconciliation — teaching that Esau, his heart turned by God, ran to embrace Jacob in love and tears.
5:
"And he lifted up his eyes, and saw the women and the children; and said, Who are those with thee? And he said, The children which God hath graciously given thy servant." The witnesses note Esau asking about the family and Jacob ascribing them to God's grace. Gill notes that, after the salutation, Esau looked and saw the women and children, Jacob's two wives, two handmaids, and twelve children. The witnesses note Esau asking who the women and children were, and Jacob answering that they were the children God had graciously given him; the gracious confession that ascribed his family to God's free gift — that Esau, his anger gone, turns with kindly interest to Jacob's household: "Who are those with thee? And he said, The children which God hath graciously given thy servant"; the witnesses note the beauty of Jacob's answer — asked about his children, he does not boast of them as his own achievement but ascribes them wholly to God: "the children which God hath graciously given thy servant"; the witnesses note the two graces in the reply — the owning of God as the giver ("God hath graciously given"), that his children were God's free and gracious gift, not his own getting; and the continued humility ("thy servant"), Jacob still using the lowly title before Esau; the witnesses note that Jacob seizes even this question to give glory to God, confessing his family as undeserved mercy; so that Jacob ascribes his children to the gracious gift of God; The children which God hath graciously given thy servant. The witnesses note Who are those with thee? The witnesses note The children which God hath graciously given thy servant.

The witnesses note Jacob, asked about his family, ascribing them wholly to God — "the children which God hath graciously given thy servant"; owning God as the giver and keeping his lowly title before Esau.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob ascribing his children to God's grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, asked about his family, owned them as God's gracious gift, not his own getting, with continued humility. The witnesses note the who are those: "Who are those with thee?" — Gill notes Esau's kindly interest; the who are those; the Esau; the kindly; the interest; the asks. The expositors observe the the women and children: "saw the women and the children" — the two wives, handmaids, children; the the women and children; the the wives; the the handmaids; the the children; the the household. The witnesses press the God hath given: "The children which God hath graciously given" — ascribed to God; the God hath given; the ascribed; the to God; the the giver; the gift. The expositors draw the lesson on the graciously: "graciously given" — God's free and gracious gift; the graciously; the free gift; the gracious; the undeserved; the mercy. The witnesses observe the thy servant: "thy servant" — continued humility; the thy servant; the humility; the lowly title; the before Esau; the humble. The expositors note the gives glory: Jacob gives glory to God; the gives glory; the glory; the to God; the confesses; the mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Who are those with thee, the The children which God hath graciously given thy servant, and the gracious confession. The verse thus records Jacob ascribing his children to God's grace — teaching that Jacob owned his family as God's gracious gift, with continued humility.
6:
"Then the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and they bowed themselves." The witnesses note the handmaids and their children drawing near and bowing to Esau. Keil and Delitzsch note the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and bowed themselves. Gill notes these were Bilhah and her two sons Dan and Naphtali, and Zilpah and her two sons Gad and Asher, being foremost and next to Jacob. The witnesses note the handmaids and their children coming near and bowing themselves to Esau; the first company doing reverence, the orderly presentation of the family begun — that the family now comes forward in the order Jacob had set, the foremost first: "the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and they bowed themselves"; the witnesses note the orderly approach — the handmaids (Bilhah and Zilpah) and their four sons (Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher), being placed foremost, "came near" first and "bowed themselves" before Esau in respect; the witnesses note that the whole family followed Jacob's own example of humble reverence, each company in turn coming near and bowing to Esau as to a lord; the witnesses note the harmony of the household in this submission, all uniting in the homage Jacob had begun, that the reconciliation might be complete and the honour to Esau full; so that the handmaids and their children come near and bow before Esau; they bowed themselves. The witnesses note the handmaidens came near, they and their children. The witnesses note and they bowed themselves.

The witnesses note the handmaids and their children, placed foremost, coming near first and bowing to Esau — the family following Jacob's own example of humble reverence, the orderly presentation begun.

So the witnesses make the verse the first company doing reverence to Esau. The lesson the expositors draw is that the handmaids and their children, foremost in order, came near and bowed before Esau, following Jacob's humble example. The witnesses note the came near: "the handmaidens came near, they and their children" — Gill names Bilhah and Zilpah's sons; the came near; the the handmaids; the their children; the foremost; the first. The expositors observe the the four sons: Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher; the the four sons; the Dan; the Naphtali; the Gad; the Asher. The witnesses press the bowed themselves: "and they bowed themselves" — bowed in respect; the bowed themselves; the bowed; the in respect; the before Esau; the homage. The expositors draw the lesson on the Jacob's example: following Jacob's own example; the Jacob's example; the his example; the reverence; the bowing; the humble. The witnesses observe the each in turn: each company in turn came and bowed; the each in turn; the in turn; the came near; the bowed; the orderly. The expositors note the harmony: the harmony of the household in submission; the harmony; the the household; the submission; the united; the homage. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the handmaidens came near they and their children, the and they bowed themselves, and the reverence. The verse thus records the first company doing reverence — teaching that the handmaids and their children came near and bowed before Esau, following Jacob's humble example.
7:
"And Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves: and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves." The witnesses note Leah and her children, then Joseph and Rachel, coming near and bowing to Esau. Gill notes Leah's children were seven, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah, six sons and one daughter. The witnesses note Leah and her children bowing next, and last Joseph and Rachel coming near and bowing; the whole family in turn doing homage, the most beloved presented last — that the presentation is completed by the remaining companies: "Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves: and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves"; the witnesses note the order kept to the end — after the handmaids, "Leah also with her children" (her seven, six sons and Dinah) "came near, and bowed themselves," and last of all "Joseph near and Rachel" (the most beloved, hindmost and safest) came and "bowed themselves" before Esau; the witnesses note the touching detail that Joseph is named before Rachel, "Joseph near and Rachel" — some seeing in it that the boy went before his mother, perhaps to shield her, a small mark of his forwardness even then; the witnesses note that the whole family, from the least honoured to the most beloved, united in the same humble reverence to Esau; so that all the household, Rachel and Joseph last, bow before Esau in turn; after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves. The witnesses note Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves. The witnesses note and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves.

The witnesses note the presentation completed — Leah and her children, then Joseph and Rachel last and safest — the whole family from least to most beloved uniting in the same humble reverence to Esau.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole family doing homage to Esau in turn. The lesson the expositors draw is that, in the appointed order, Leah and her children, then Joseph and Rachel last, came near and bowed before Esau. The witnesses note the Leah and her children: "Leah also with her children came near" — Gill names her seven; the Leah and her children; the her seven; the six sons; the Dinah; the her children. The expositors observe the bowed themselves: "and bowed themselves" — bowed in respect; the bowed themselves; the bowed; the in respect; the before Esau; the homage. The witnesses press the Joseph and Rachel: "after came Joseph near and Rachel" — last and most beloved; the Joseph and Rachel; the last; the most beloved; the hindmost; the safest. The expositors draw the lesson on the Joseph before Rachel: Joseph named before Rachel; the Joseph before Rachel; the named first; the the boy; the before his mother; the forward. The witnesses observe the order kept: the order kept to the end; the order kept; the the order; the to the end; the in turn; the appointed. The expositors note the all united: the whole family united in reverence; the all united; the the family; the united; the reverence; the from least to most. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Leah also with her children came near and bowed themselves, the and after came Joseph near and Rachel and they bowed themselves, and the whole family's homage. The verse thus records the whole family doing homage — teaching that, in the appointed order, Leah and her children, then Joseph and Rachel last, bowed before Esau.
8:
"And he said, What meanest thou by all this drove which I met? And he said, These are to find grace in the sight of my lord." The witnesses note Esau asking about the droves and Jacob explaining they were to find favour. Gill notes Esau asked not as being ignorant of the design of it, for the drovers had told him, but he chose not to take the present without showing reluctance. The witnesses note Esau asking what Jacob meant by the droves he met, and Jacob answering they were to find grace in his sight; the courteous exchange over the gift, Jacob owning his purpose to win his brother's favour — that Esau, having met the droves on the way, now asks their meaning: "What meanest thou by all this drove which I met?"; the witnesses note that Esau's question was not from ignorance — the drovers had already told him whose the gift was and why; but, as Gill notes, Esau out of courtesy and a show of reluctance would have Jacob himself declare it, perhaps meaning to decline so large a present; the witnesses note Jacob's plain and humble answer, "These are to find grace in the sight of my lord," owning openly that the gift was sent to win Esau's favour and seal their peace; the witnesses note the candour of Jacob, hiding nothing of his purpose, that the present was an offering of goodwill to his brother; so that Jacob declares the droves were sent to find grace with Esau; These are to find grace in the sight of my lord. The witnesses note What meanest thou by all this drove which I met? The witnesses note These are to find grace in the sight of my lord.

The witnesses note Esau asking the meaning of the droves and Jacob owning plainly they were "to find grace in the sight of my lord" — the courteous exchange over the gift, Jacob hiding nothing of his purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse the exchange over the present's purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau asked the meaning of the droves and Jacob openly owned they were sent to win his brother's favour. The witnesses note the what meanest thou: "What meanest thou by all this drove which I met?" — Gill notes not from ignorance; the what meanest thou; the not ignorance; the the drovers told; the courtesy; the asks. The expositors observe the show of reluctance: a show of reluctance to take it; the show of reluctance; the reluctance; the decline; the courtesy; the large gift. The witnesses press the find grace: "These are to find grace in the sight of my lord" — to win favour; the find grace; the win favour; the goodwill; the the gift; the seal peace. The expositors draw the lesson on the plain answer: Jacob's plain, humble answer; the plain answer; the plain; the humble; the owns; the openly. The witnesses observe the my lord: "in the sight of my lord" — continued humility; the my lord; the humility; the lowly; the before Esau; the humble. The expositors note the candour: the candour of Jacob, hiding nothing; the candour; the hiding nothing; the his purpose; the goodwill; the open. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the What meanest thou by all this drove which I met, the These are to find grace in the sight of my lord, and the exchange over the gift. The verse thus records the exchange over the present's purpose — teaching that Jacob openly owned the droves were sent to win his brother's favour.
9:
"And Esau said, I have enough, my brother; keep that thou hast unto thyself." The witnesses note Esau's generous declining of the gift, calling Jacob brother. Gill notes Esau said, "I have much," and stood in no need of the present, having much more than Jacob, and so would have Jacob keep it. The witnesses note Esau saying he had enough and bidding Jacob keep his gift; the generous refusal of a softened brother, content with his own — that Esau, his heart full, at first declines the present: "I have enough, my brother; keep that thou hast unto thyself"; the witnesses note the change in Esau's spirit — the once-vengeful brother now speaks with warmth and generosity, calling Jacob "my brother" (the word of restored kinship) and declining the gift with "I have enough" (literally "I have much"), content with his own and not wishing to take from Jacob; the witnesses note the contrast often drawn — that the worldly Esau says "I have enough," while Jacob (v.11) says God has dealt graciously and given him all; both are content, but Esau with his much, Jacob with God's grace; the witnesses note that Esau's refusal sprang not from coldness but from a brotherly generosity, unwilling to be enriched at Jacob's cost; so that Esau, content and softened, would have Jacob keep his own; keep that thou hast unto thyself. The witnesses note I have enough, my brother. The witnesses note keep that thou hast unto thyself.

The witnesses note Esau's generous refusal — "I have enough, my brother" — the once-vengeful brother now warm and content, declining the gift in brotherly generosity, unwilling to be enriched at Jacob's cost.

So the witnesses make the verse Esau's generous declining of the gift. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau, softened and content, at first declined the present, calling Jacob brother and bidding him keep his own. The witnesses note the I have enough: "I have enough, my brother" — Gill notes "I have much"; the I have enough; the I have much; the content; the no need; the enough. The expositors observe the my brother: "my brother" — restored kinship; the my brother; the kinship; the restored; the warm; the brotherly. The witnesses press the keep that thou hast: "keep that thou hast unto thyself" — declines the gift; the keep that thou hast; the declines; the the gift; the thy own; the refuses. The expositors draw the lesson on the the contrast: Esau "I have much," Jacob God's grace; the the contrast; the I have much; the God's grace; the both content; the the worldly. The witnesses observe the not coldness: the refusal from generosity, not coldness; the not coldness; the generosity; the brotherly; the not cold; the warm. The expositors note the not at Jacob's cost: unwilling to be enriched at Jacob's cost; the not at Jacob's cost; the unwilling; the enriched; the his cost; the generous. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I have enough my brother, the keep that thou hast unto thyself, and Esau's refusal. The verse thus records Esau's generous declining — teaching that Esau, softened and content, declined the present, calling Jacob brother.
10:
"And Jacob said, Nay, I pray thee, if now I have found grace in thy sight, then receive my present at my hand: for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me." The witnesses note Jacob urging the gift and likening Esau's gracious face to the face of God. Adam Clarke notes Jacob could not be certain he had found favour with Esau unless the present were received, for the receiving of it was the proof of reconciliation. The witnesses note Jacob pressing Esau to receive the gift, saying he had seen his face as the face of God, who was pleased with him; the earnest urging of the present, the gift the very proof of reconciliation, Esau's kindness likened to God's favour — that Jacob will not be refused, but presses the gift with a remarkable plea: "if now I have found grace in thy sight, then receive my present at my hand: for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me"; the witnesses note Jacob's reasoning — the acceptance of the gift would be the proof and seal of reconciliation (as Clarke notes), so Jacob urges Esau to receive it as the token that grace was indeed found; the witnesses note the striking comparison, "I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God"; Jacob, fresh from Peniel where he saw "the face of God" and lived, sees in Esau's softened face a reflection of God's own favour, and as God was pleased with him so Esau "wast pleased with me"; as though I had seen the face of God. The witnesses note receive my present at my hand. The witnesses note for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me.

The witnesses note Jacob pressing the gift as the seal of reconciliation, and likening Esau's softened face to "the face of God" — the reconciled brother's countenance mirroring the favour Jacob met at Peniel.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob urging the gift and likening Esau's face to God's. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob pressed the present as the proof of reconciliation, seeing in Esau's gracious face a reflection of God's own favour. The witnesses note the receive my present: "receive my present at my hand" — Clarke notes the proof of reconciliation; the receive my present; the the proof; the the seal; the urges. The expositors observe the found grace: "if now I have found grace in thy sight" — the token of grace; the found grace; the the token; the favour. The witnesses press the seen thy face: "I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God" — Esau's face like God's; the seen thy face; the like God's; the softened; the gracious. The expositors draw the lesson on the Peniel: fresh from Peniel where he saw God's face; the Peniel; the saw and lived; the mirrors. The witnesses observe the thou wast pleased: "and thou wast pleased with me" — as God was pleased; the thou wast pleased; the as God; the reconciled. The expositors note the favour shining: God's favour shining in his brother's face; the favour shining; the his brother's face; the mirrors. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for therefore I have seen thy face as though I had seen the face of God, and the urging of the gift. The verse thus records Jacob urging the gift and likening Esau's face to God's — teaching that Jacob pressed the present as the proof of reconciliation, seeing God's favour in his brother's face.
11:
"Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee; because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough. And he urged him, and he took it." The witnesses note Jacob calling the gift his blessing and pressing it on Esau, who at last took it. Gill notes the present was part of what God had blessed Jacob with, which from a generous spirit he gave his brother, wishing God's blessing to go with it. The witnesses note Jacob calling the gift his blessing, urging Esau because God had dealt graciously and he had enough, and Esau taking it; the gift pressed as a blessing, Jacob content in God's abundant grace — that Jacob crowns his urging with the deepest reason: "Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee; because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough. And he urged him, and he took it"; the witnesses note the rich naming of the gift — Jacob calls it "my blessing," a portion of the blessing God had given him, freely passed on to his brother with a wish of God's favour; the witnesses note the ground of Jacob's generosity, "because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough" — Jacob can give freely because God has given him all; and the contrast with Esau (v.9) is complete: Esau said "I have much," but Jacob says "I have enough" because "God hath dealt graciously," resting his sufficiency in God's grace, not in mere abundance; the witnesses note that Jacob "urged him, and he took it," the gift at last accepted, the reconciliation sealed; so that Jacob gives his blessing, content in grace, and Esau receives it; And he urged him, and he took it. The witnesses note Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee. The witnesses note because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough.

The witnesses note Jacob pressing the gift as "my blessing," content because "God hath dealt graciously" — his sufficiency resting in God's grace, not mere abundance; and Esau at last accepting it, the reconciliation sealed.

So the witnesses make the verse the gift given as a blessing, content in God's grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob pressed the gift as a blessing, content because God had dealt graciously, and Esau accepted it, sealing the peace. The witnesses note the my blessing: "Take, I pray thee, my blessing" — Gill notes part of God's blessing; the my blessing; the God's blessing; the a portion; the freely given; the the gift. The expositors observe the dealt graciously: "because God hath dealt graciously with me" — the ground of his giving; the dealt graciously; the the ground; the God's grace; the freely; the gives. The witnesses press the I have enough: "and because I have enough" — content in grace; the I have enough; the content; the in grace; the sufficiency; the all. The expositors draw the lesson on the the contrast: Esau "I have much," Jacob "I have enough" by grace; the the contrast; the I have much; the by grace; the God's grace; the rests. The witnesses observe the urged him: "he urged him, and he took it" — the gift accepted; the urged him; the accepted; the took it; the the seal; the reconciliation. The expositors note the sealed peace: the reconciliation sealed; the sealed peace; the the peace; the accepted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the because God hath dealt graciously with me and because I have enough, and the gift given. The verse thus records the gift given as a blessing — teaching that Jacob pressed it content in God's grace, and Esau accepted it, sealing the peace.
12:
"And he said, Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee." The witnesses note Esau proposing to travel together and lead the way. Keil and Delitzsch note Esau said, "Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee," offering to accompany Jacob. Gill notes Esau invited Jacob to go with him to Seir, where he lived, to stop and refresh. The witnesses note Esau proposing that they journey together, he going before Jacob; the brotherly offer to accompany and lead, springing from Esau's restored goodwill — that Esau, the reconciliation now full, would have the brothers travel on together: "Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee"; the witnesses note the warmth of the offer — Esau, his heart wholly won, proposes that they "take our journey... together," and that he himself "go before thee," leading the way and perhaps acting as Jacob's guide and protector toward Seir, where he dwelt; the witnesses note the genuine kindness in this, that Esau wished to keep his recovered brother by him, to honour and escort him; the witnesses note that the offer, though kindly meant, raised a difficulty for Jacob, whose tender flocks and children could not keep pace with Esau's swift band, and whose path lay elsewhere; so that Esau, in brotherly love, offers to journey on with Jacob and lead the way; I will go before thee. The witnesses note Let us take our journey, and let us go. The witnesses note and I will go before thee.

The witnesses note Esau's warm offer to travel on together and lead the way — his heart wholly won, wishing to keep his recovered brother by him; a genuine kindness, though it raised a difficulty for Jacob.

So the witnesses make the verse Esau's brotherly offer to journey together. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau, his goodwill full, proposed to travel on with Jacob and lead the way, wishing to keep his brother by him. The witnesses note the take our journey: "Let us take our journey, and let us go" — Keil notes the offer to accompany; the take our journey; the together; the accompany; the the offer; the journey. The expositors observe the to Seir: Esau invited him toward Seir; the to Seir; the Seir; the his home; the refresh; the invited. The witnesses press the go before thee: "I will go before thee" — leading the way; the go before thee; the leading; the the way; the guide; the protector. The expositors draw the lesson on the warmth: the warmth of the offer; the warmth; the kindly; the his heart won; the genuine; the love. The witnesses observe the keep his brother: wishing to keep his brother by him; the keep his brother; the keep; the his brother; the honour; the escort. The expositors note the a difficulty: the offer raised a difficulty for Jacob; the a difficulty; the the difficulty; the tender flocks; the the children; the the path. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Let us take our journey and let us go, the and I will go before thee, and Esau's offer. The verse thus records Esau's brotherly offer — teaching that Esau, his goodwill full, proposed to travel on with Jacob and lead the way.
13:
"And he said unto him, My lord knoweth that the children are tender, and the flocks and herds with young are with me: and if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die." The witnesses note Jacob's reason for not keeping pace with Esau — the tender children and young flocks. Gill notes the eldest child was but thirteen and the youngest about six, and Esau might see by their stature they were young and tender, not able to bear hard travel. The witnesses note Jacob explaining that the children were tender and the flocks with young, so that overdriving one day would kill the flock; the prudent and considerate reason for declining Esau's pace — that Jacob, with respectful care, declines to keep Esau's swift pace: "My lord knoweth that the children are tender, and the flocks and herds with young are with me: and if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die"; the witnesses note the wisdom and tenderness of the plea — Jacob points to "the children" (young and tender, the eldest but thirteen, the youngest about six) and "the flocks and herds with young" (the nursing and breeding cattle), all of which would suffer or perish if driven hard; the witnesses note the shepherd's care in the warning, "if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die" — a gentle and reasonable ground for travelling at his own slower pace; the witnesses note that Jacob, while declining the escort, does so with all courtesy, calling Esau "my lord" and appealing to his understanding; so that Jacob gives the tender flocks and children as his reason for a slower journey; if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die. The witnesses note My lord knoweth that the children are tender. The witnesses note if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die.

The witnesses note Jacob declining Esau's swift pace with a shepherd's care — the tender children and nursing flocks would perish if overdriven one day; a prudent, courteous, reasonable plea.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's considerate reason for a slower journey. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, pointing to the tender children and young flocks that overdriving would kill, courteously declined to keep Esau's swift pace. The witnesses note the children are tender: "the children are tender" — Gill notes the eldest thirteen, youngest six; the children are tender; the young; the tender; the the eldest; the the youngest. The expositors observe the flocks with young: "the flocks and herds with young are with me" — the nursing cattle; the flocks with young; the nursing; the breeding; the the cattle; the with young. The witnesses press the overdrive: "if men should overdrive them one day" — driven hard; the overdrive; the driven hard; the one day; the the pace; the suffer. The expositors draw the lesson on the all die: "all the flock will die" — the flock would perish; the all die; the perish; the the flock; the die; the harm. The witnesses observe the shepherd's care: the shepherd's care in the warning; the shepherd's care; the care; the the shepherd; the the warning; the tender. The expositors note the courtesy: he declines with all courtesy; the courtesy; the courteous; the my lord; the respectful; the appeals. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the My lord knoweth that the children are tender, the if men should overdrive them one day all the flock will die, and the considerate reason. The verse thus records Jacob's considerate reason — teaching that Jacob, pointing to the tender children and young flocks, courteously declined Esau's swift pace.
14:
"Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant: and I will lead on softly, according as the cattle that goeth before me and the children be able to endure, until I come unto my lord unto Seir." The witnesses note Jacob asking Esau to go on ahead while he follows softly toward Seir. Gill notes Jacob desired in a respectful manner that Esau would not keep his pace in complaisance to him, but proceed on with his men, and Jacob would follow after as fast as he could. The witnesses note Jacob asking Esau to go before while he leads on softly as the cattle and children can bear, until he comes to Seir; the courteous declining of the escort, Jacob to follow at his own gentle pace — that Jacob proposes the solution: "Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant: and I will lead on softly, according as the cattle... and the children be able to endure, until I come unto my lord unto Seir"; the witnesses note the courtesy and the prudence — Jacob begs Esau to "pass over before" (go on ahead at his own swift pace, not waiting for Jacob), while Jacob himself would "lead on softly" (travel gently, at the pace the cattle and children could bear); the witnesses note the difficulty some have raised — that Jacob says "until I come unto my lord unto Seir," yet (as the next verses show) he went not to Seir but to Succoth and Shechem; some take this as Jacob's prudent caution, others as a courteous intention later altered; whatever the case, Jacob wisely sought to part in peace and go his own way; I will lead on softly... until I come unto my lord unto Seir. The witnesses note Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant. The witnesses note I will lead on softly... until I come unto my lord unto Seir.

The witnesses note Jacob courteously declining the escort — begging Esau to go ahead while he leads on softly toward Seir; some reading "until I come unto Seir" as prudent caution, since he went instead to Succoth.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's courteous parting from Esau's company. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob begged Esau to go on ahead while he followed at a gentle pace, wisely seeking to part in peace and go his own way. The witnesses note the pass over before: "Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant" — Gill notes go on at his own pace; the pass over before; the go ahead; the not wait. The expositors observe the lead on softly: "I will lead on softly" — at the cattle's pace; the lead on softly; the gently; the the pace. The witnesses press the children endure: "according as the children be able to endure" — as they can bear; the children endure; the can bear; the gently. The expositors draw the lesson on the unto Seir: "until I come unto my lord unto Seir" — yet he went to Succoth; the unto Seir; the yet Succoth; the not Seir. The witnesses present the debate: the witnesses note some read this as prudent caution, others as a courteous intention later altered. The witnesses observe the part in peace: Jacob sought to part in peace; the part in peace; the his own way; the wisely. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will lead on softly until I come unto my lord unto Seir, and the courteous parting. The verse thus records Jacob's courteous parting — teaching that Jacob begged Esau to go ahead while he followed softly, seeking to part in peace.
15:
"And Esau said, Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me. And he said, What needeth it? let me find grace in the sight of my lord." The witnesses note Esau offering an escort and Jacob graciously declining it. John Wesley notes Esau offers some of his men to be Jacob's guard and convoy, but Jacob humbly declines it. Gill notes Esau offered to leave some of his folk to show Jacob the way and guard him on the road. The witnesses note Esau offering to leave some of his men with Jacob, and Jacob declining, asking only to find grace in his sight; the kindly offer of an escort gently refused, Jacob desiring only goodwill — that Esau, still wishing to honour and protect his brother, makes a further offer: "Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me"; the witnesses note the continued kindness of Esau — having failed to keep Jacob with him, he would at least "leave... some of the folk" (some of his four hundred men) as a guard and guide for Jacob's road, and an honour to him; the witnesses note Jacob's gracious refusal, "What needeth it?" — Jacob, not wishing to be beholden to Esau's men nor to travel under their watch, courteously declines, asking only the one thing he truly desired: "let me find grace in the sight of my lord"; the witnesses note that Jacob sought from Esau not an escort but his favour, content with the reconciliation and the goodwill restored; so that Jacob declines the escort, desiring only his brother's grace; let me find grace in the sight of my lord. The witnesses note Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me. The witnesses note What needeth it? let me find grace in the sight of my lord.

The witnesses note Esau's kindly offer of an escort and Jacob's gracious refusal — desiring not a guard but only his brother's favour; content with the reconciliation and the goodwill restored.

So the witnesses make the verse Esau's offer of an escort gently declined. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau offered some of his men as a guard, but Jacob declined, asking only to find grace in his brother's sight. The witnesses note the leave some folk: "Let me now leave with thee some of the folk" — Wesley notes a guard and convoy; the leave some folk; the a guard; the a convoy; the his men; the offers. The expositors observe the continued kindness: Esau's continued kindness; the continued kindness; the kindness; the to honour; the to protect; the offers. The witnesses press the what needeth it: "What needeth it?" — Jacob's gracious refusal; the what needeth it; the refusal; the declines; the gracious; the courteous. The expositors draw the lesson on the not beholden: not wishing to be beholden to Esau's men; the not beholden; the not beholden; the not watched; the declines; the courteous. The witnesses observe the find grace: "let me find grace in the sight of my lord" — desiring only favour; the find grace; the only favour; the his grace; the goodwill; the desired. The expositors note the content with peace: content with the reconciliation restored; the content with peace; the content; the reconciliation; the goodwill; the restored. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me, the What needeth it let me find grace in the sight of my lord, and the escort declined. The verse thus records Esau's offer gently declined — teaching that Jacob declined the guard, asking only to find grace in his brother's sight.
16:
"So Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir." The witnesses note Esau departing the same day to return to Seir. Matthew Henry notes here Jacob and Esau part friends; Esau is loving and Jacob wary, and the reconciliation, once made, is not broken. Gill notes Esau took his leave of Jacob the same day he met him and proceeded toward Seir. The witnesses note Esau returning that day on his way to Seir; the peaceful parting of the brothers, each going his own way in restored goodwill — that the meeting ends as it should, in a friendly parting: "Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir"; the witnesses note the quiet close of the great reconciliation — Esau, his errand of love accomplished and his offers kindly declined, "returned that day" toward his own country, "unto Seir," leaving Jacob to follow his own path; the witnesses note (with Matthew Henry) that "Jacob and Esau part friends," the long enmity healed, Esau loving and Jacob wary, but the peace between them real and lasting; the witnesses note the providence of God in the whole — the dreaded meeting that Jacob had feared would be his destruction ended in embrace, gift, and a peaceful parting, every fear answered by grace; so that Esau returns to Seir, and the brothers part in peace; So Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir. The witnesses note So Esau returned that day. The witnesses note on his way unto Seir.

The witnesses note Esau returning that day to Seir — the peaceful parting of the brothers, the long enmity healed; the dreaded meeting Jacob feared would destroy him ending in embrace, gift, and peace.

So the witnesses make the verse the peaceful parting of the brothers. The lesson the expositors draw is that Esau returned to Seir and the brothers parted friends, the long enmity healed and every fear answered by grace. The witnesses note the returned that day: "Esau returned that day" — Gill notes took his leave; the returned that day; the took his leave; the the same day; the departed; the toward Seir. The expositors observe the unto Seir: "on his way unto Seir" — toward his own country; the unto Seir; the his country; the his home; the toward; the returned. The witnesses press the part friends: Matthew Henry notes they part friends; the part friends; the friends; the Esau loving; the Jacob wary; the the peace. The expositors draw the lesson on the enmity healed: the long enmity healed; the enmity healed; the healed; the the enmity; the peace; the lasting. The witnesses observe the dreaded meeting: the dreaded meeting ended in peace; the dreaded meeting; the dreaded; the ended; the in peace; the embrace. The expositors note the every fear answered: every fear answered by grace; the every fear answered; the every fear; the answered; the by grace; the providence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the So Esau returned that day, the on his way unto Seir, and the peaceful parting. The verse thus records the peaceful parting — teaching that Esau returned to Seir and the brothers parted friends, every fear answered by grace.
17:
"And Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle: therefore the name of the place is called Succoth." The witnesses note Jacob journeying to Succoth, building a house and booths, whence the name. Gill notes Succoth was on the other side of Jordan, so called by anticipation, for it had its name from what follows; as yet there was no city here. The witnesses note Jacob journeying to Succoth, building a house and booths for his cattle, whence the place was named Succoth; the resting-place named for the booths Jacob made, his first settled halt after the meeting — that Jacob, parted from Esau, moves to a place of rest and gives it its name: "Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle: therefore the name of the place is called Succoth"; the witnesses note the settling of Jacob — after the long journey and the dreaded meeting now past, Jacob comes to a place where he "built him an house" (a more permanent dwelling) "and made booths for his cattle" (shelters or folds for his beasts), tokens of a longer stay; the witnesses note the origin of the name "Succoth" (which means "booths"), the place taking its name from the booths Jacob raised there; the witnesses note that the name was used "by anticipation," for as yet there was no city, only the booths Jacob made, which gave the spot its lasting name; so that Jacob settles at Succoth, named for the booths he built; the name of the place is called Succoth. The witnesses note Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle. The witnesses note therefore the name of the place is called Succoth.

The witnesses note Jacob settling at Succoth — building a house and booths for his cattle, tokens of a longer stay; the place taking its lasting name, "booths," from the shelters he raised there.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's settling at Succoth. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, parted from Esau, came to a resting-place where he built a house and booths, whence the place was named Succoth. The witnesses note the journeyed to Succoth: "Jacob journeyed to Succoth" — Gill notes beyond Jordan; the journeyed to Succoth; the beyond Jordan; the the place; the the journey; the came. The expositors observe the built a house: "built him an house" — a more permanent dwelling; the built a house; the a dwelling; the permanent; the a longer stay; the house. The witnesses press the made booths: "made booths for his cattle" — shelters for his beasts; the made booths; the shelters; the folds; the his cattle; the booths. The expositors draw the lesson on the the name: "the name of the place is called Succoth" — named for the booths; the the name; the named; the for the booths; the the spot; the lasting. The witnesses observe the means booths: "Succoth" means "booths"; the means booths; the booths; the the name; the the meaning; the the shelters. The expositors note the by anticipation: the name used by anticipation; the by anticipation; the anticipation; the no city yet; the the booths; the named. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jacob journeyed to Succoth and built him an house and made booths for his cattle, the therefore the name of the place is called Succoth, and the resting-place. The verse thus records Jacob's settling at Succoth — teaching that Jacob built a house and booths, whence the place was named Succoth.
18:
"And Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Padanaram; and pitched his tent before the city." The witnesses note Jacob coming safely to Shechem in Canaan and pitching his tent. Gill notes this was not Salem of Melchizedek nor Jerusalem, being forty miles from it, but the city of Shechem; the word may be read either as a name or "in peace." The witnesses note Jacob coming to Shalem, a city of Shechem in Canaan, from Padan-aram, and pitching his tent before the city; the safe arrival in the land of promise, Jacob come whole and in peace from his long exile — that Jacob at last reaches Canaan: "Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Padanaram; and pitched his tent before the city"; the witnesses note the great mercy in this arrival — Jacob, who twenty years before had fled from Canaan a lone fugitive, now "came... in the land of Canaan," safe back in the land of promise, "when he came from Padanaram"; the witnesses note the reading of "Shalem" — some take it as the name of a place (a city of Shechem), others render it "in peace" or "whole," that Jacob came "safe" and "in peace" to Shechem, his body whole (the limp notwithstanding), his family entire, his goods preserved; the witnesses note that Jacob "pitched his tent before the city," settling near Shechem in the land God had promised; so that Jacob comes safe and in peace to Canaan and pitches by Shechem; pitched his tent before the city. The witnesses note Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan. The witnesses note and pitched his tent before the city.

The witnesses note Jacob's safe arrival in Canaan at Shechem — come whole and "in peace" from his long exile; the fugitive of twenty years before now back in the land of promise, pitching his tent by the city.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's safe arrival in the land of promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob came safe and in peace to Shechem in Canaan, the fugitive of twenty years now home, pitching his tent near the city. The witnesses note the came to Shechem: "Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem" — Gill notes not Salem of Melchizedek; the came to Shechem; the not Salem; the the city; the of Shechem; the came. The expositors observe the in Canaan: "which is in the land of Canaan" — the land of promise; the in Canaan; the the land; the of promise; the back; the home. The witnesses press the from Padanaram: "when he came from Padanaram" — from his long exile; the from Padanaram; the his exile; the twenty years; the the fugitive; the returned. The expositors draw the lesson on the Shalem or in peace: "Shalem" as a name or "in peace"; the Shalem or in peace; the the name; the in peace; the whole; the safe. The witnesses observe the came safe: Jacob came safe and whole; the came safe; the safe; the whole; the his family; the preserved. The expositors note the pitched his tent: "pitched his tent before the city" — settling near Shechem; the pitched his tent; the settled; the near Shechem; the the tent; the the city. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jacob came to Shalem a city of Shechem which is in the land of Canaan, the and pitched his tent before the city, and the safe arrival. The verse thus records Jacob's safe arrival in the land of promise — teaching that Jacob came safe and in peace to Shechem in Canaan, the fugitive now home.
19:
"And he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent, at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem's father, for an hundred pieces of money." The witnesses note Jacob buying the parcel of ground where he pitched his tent. Keil and Delitzsch note Jacob bought the parcel of field where he had spread his tent from the children of Hamor for a hundred pieces of money. Gill notes Jacob bought a part, not the whole, of the field, though he was heir of the whole country, because the time was not yet come for him to take possession. The witnesses note Jacob buying a parcel of the field where his tent was spread from the sons of Hamor for a hundred pieces of money; the lawful purchase of a foothold in the promised land, the patriarch buying what was already his by promise — that Jacob secures a lawful title to the ground: "he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent, at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem's father, for an hundred pieces of money"; the witnesses note the significance of this purchase — Jacob "bought a parcel" (a portion, not the whole) of the field where his tent stood, paying "an hundred pieces of money" to "the children of Hamor," the lawful owners; the witnesses note the irony and the faith in it — that Jacob, who was by God's promise heir of all Canaan, yet buys a small parcel with money, because (as Gill notes) the time for his seed to possess the land was not yet come, and he would hold even this foothold by honest purchase; the witnesses note that this parcel became a lasting possession, where Joseph's bones were later laid; so that Jacob buys a foothold in the land that was his by promise; for an hundred pieces of money. The witnesses note he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent. The witnesses note at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem's father, for an hundred pieces of money.

The witnesses note Jacob buying a parcel of the field for a hundred pieces — the heir of all Canaan by promise yet buying a small foothold with money, since the time for his seed to possess the land was not yet come.

So the witnesses make the verse the lawful purchase of a foothold in the land. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, heir of all Canaan by promise, bought a small parcel by honest purchase, the time of possession not yet come. The witnesses note the bought a parcel: "he bought a parcel of a field" — Gill notes a part, not the whole; the bought a parcel; the a part; the not the whole; the the field; the bought. The witnesses press the children of Hamor: "at the hand of the children of Hamor" — the lawful owners; the children of Hamor; the the owners; the lawful. The expositors draw the lesson on the hundred pieces: "for an hundred pieces of money" — honest purchase; the hundred pieces; the the price; the money; the honest; the purchase. The witnesses observe the heir yet buys: heir of all yet buys a parcel; the heir yet buys; the of all Canaan; the by promise. The expositors note the time not come: the time of possession not yet come; the time not come; the not yet; the future. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the at the hand of the children of Hamor Shechem's father for an hundred pieces of money, and the lawful purchase. The verse thus records the lawful purchase of a foothold — teaching that Jacob, heir of all Canaan by promise, bought a small parcel by honest purchase.
20:
"And he erected there an altar, and called it Elelohe-Israel." The witnesses note Jacob raising an altar at Shechem and naming it El-elohe-Israel. Gill notes Jacob erected an altar to offer sacrifice to God by way of thanksgiving for the many mercies he had received since he left Canaan, and especially for his safe return. The witnesses note Jacob erecting an altar and calling it El-elohe-Israel, "God, the God of Israel"; the altar of thanksgiving that confessed the God who had kept him, sealed with his own new name — that Jacob, safely home, crowns his arrival with worship: "he erected there an altar, and called it Elelohe-Israel"; the witnesses note the act and the name — Jacob "erected... an altar" to offer sacrifice to God in thanksgiving for all the mercies of his long journey, above all for his safe return to Canaan; and he "called it Elelohe-Israel," which means "God, the God of Israel"; the witnesses note the rich confession in the name — Jacob owns God as his own God under his own new name "Israel" (the prince who prevailed at Peniel), the God who had been the God of Abraham and Isaac now confessed as "the God of Israel," his God by covenant and by the night's wrestling; the witnesses note that this altar marks Jacob's grateful return to the worship of God in the promised land, sealing his pilgrimage with praise; so that Jacob raises an altar to the God of Israel, in thanksgiving for his keeping; he called it Elelohe-Israel. The witnesses note he erected there an altar. The witnesses note and called it Elelohe-Israel.

The witnesses note Jacob raising an altar of thanksgiving and naming it El-elohe-Israel, "God, the God of Israel" — owning God as his own under his new name, confessing the God who had kept him through all.

So the witnesses make the verse the altar of thanksgiving to the God of Israel. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, safely home, raised an altar in thanksgiving and named it for God as his own God under the name Israel. The witnesses note the erected an altar: "he erected there an altar" — Gill notes for thanksgiving; the erected an altar; the for thanksgiving; the sacrifice; the the mercies; the raised. The expositors observe the for his mercies: for the mercies of his journey and safe return; the for his mercies; the the mercies; the the journey; the safe return; the thanksgiving. The witnesses press the El-elohe-Israel: "called it Elelohe-Israel" — "God, the God of Israel"; the El-elohe-Israel; the God; the the God of Israel; the the name; the confession.
